evidence_quotes.quote: that customers should have protections from publishers destroying videogames that have already been sold to consumers just because they want to.
when they stop updates and support for a game to still keep it running in a limited capability that it still is overall playable and not lost to history

In short payment processors have no right to dictate what a seller can or cannot sell and a buyer can or cannot buy as long as it is done legally with the following of all laws, rules and regulations.

paragraphs.text: Hello EU commission or whoever is reading my feedback, i took notice of the Digital Fairness act due to youtuber Ross Scott from Accursed farms and i like the act and suggestion. More so i would like to suggest a few things myself such as: that customers should have protections from publishers destroying videogames that have already been sold to consumers just because they want to. While i myself did not have a similar experience i did hear about the "The Crew" thing i think publishers should, when they stop updates and support for a game to still keep it running in a limited capability that it still is overall playable and not lost to history, as that means a loss of both what the consumer paid for and a loss of videogame itself and it's history and just takes from the consumers. Second thing i wanted to mention is the recent upheaval over payment processors such as Visa and Mastercard forcing sites like Itch.io and Steam to delete a group of games off their platforms or the payment processors, which are widely used will leave or some further sanction, thus forcing the companies like Valve to do it. That is a major breach of consumer rights, as payment processors whose main and perhaps only job is to process payments, refuse to do so if the product like a videogame the consumer is buying is not liked by them, or go further to wipe the game off the platform. While yes, those games while being NSFW are still on the platforms legally, with the creators and host platform not breaking any laws and the group influencing the payment processors, Collective Shout, having records of going after games like GTA and Detroit: Become Human, which could indicate they might try or even succeed with banning games like that, not to mention what is classified as "NSFW" is being vague which could result in even just games that represent the LGBTQ+ community in a way getting banned too. In short payment processors have no right to dictate what a seller can or cannot sell and a buyer can or cannot buy as long as it is done legally with the following of all laws, rules and regulations. I hope this and other feedback will do you good in understanding what we are fighting against and what is at stake, thank you and with the help of the EU and it's laws, feedbacks and citizen initiatives which has made it possible for the consumers to fight back in the first place, like with the SKG initiative which has reached 1.4 million signatures indicating that the motivation and morale is there and that people will not stand idly by while game publishers and payment processors take their rights away. Thank you for taking you time to read this and providing us comsumers with the possibility to do something. I hope that mainly the second thing gets taken care of with this act, but doing both is even better. Thank you and the EU for making this possible
